Summary: This study aimed to evaluate and compare the performance of LI-RADS and radiomics from multiparametric CE-MRI in the preoperative prediction of MVI status in solitary HCC < 5 cm. The study found that incomplete tumor capsule and corona enhancement on preoperative MRI were related to MVI. The combination model, including clinical and radiomic features, exhibited superior diagnostic performance to the clinical model and single-sequence radiomic models in predicting MVI.

Item1.reason: The study does not explicitly mention adherence to radiomics or machine learning-specific checklists or guidelines.

Item2.reason: The study clearly defines inclusion and exclusion criteria for patient enrollment, leading to a representative study sample.

Item3.reason: The reference standard is histopathological diagnosis of MVI, which is a robust and well-established standard.

Item4.reason: The study was conducted at a single institution.

Item5.reason: The study uses CE-MRI, which is a clinically translatable imaging technique for HCC diagnosis and staging.

Item6.reason: The study reports the image acquisition parameters, including TR, TE, flip angle, FOV, matrix, section thickness, and gap.

Item7.reason: The study specifies that CE-MRI was performed within 30 days preoperatively, which is appropriate for validating the presence or absence of MVI at the time of imaging.

Condition1.reason: The study includes tumor segmentation as part of the radiomic analysis workflow.

Condition2.reason: The study mentions that the ROI was drawn manually by two radiologists, so the segmentation is not fully automated.

Item8.reason: The study describes the segmentation methodology, including the use of B-spline interpolation for resampling and gray-level discretization. It also mentions that the ROI was drawn along the tumor margin layer by layer to generate a 3D-ROI.

Item9.reason: The segmentation was performed manually, not fully automated, so this item is not applicable.

Item10.reason: The test set segmentation masks were produced by two readers, not a single reader or automated tool.

Condition3.reason: The study extracts hand-crafted radiomic features using PyRadiomics.

Item11.reason: The study mentions preprocessing steps, including resampling the voxel size to 1x1x1 mm3 using B-spline interpolation and gray-level intensity discretization with a fixed bin width of 25.

Item12.reason: The study used PyRadiomics v.2.1.2 for feature extraction, which is a standardized software.

Item13.reason: The study describes the feature types (first-order statistics, morphological features, GLCM, GLSZM, GLRLM, NGTDM, and GLDM features) and mentions that the same series of radiomic features were extracted from T2WI/FS, AP, PVP, and DP images.

Condition4.reason: The study includes tabular data in the form of extracted radiomic features.

Condition5.reason: The study does not use an end-to-end deep learning pipeline.

Item14.reason: The study uses ICC analysis to assess the reliability and stability of each feature and retains only those features exhibiting ICC values greater than 0.75.

Item15.reason: The study uses Pearson's correlation to remove redundant features with mutual correlation coefficients > 0.9 and LASSO for feature selection.

Item16.reason: The study does not explicitly discuss the appropriateness of dimensionality compared to data size.

Item17.reason: The study does not use an end-to-end deep learning pipeline, so this item is not applicable.

Item18.reason: The study mentions that patients were randomly stratified into training, internal validation, and independent test cohorts. However, it does not explicitly state whether the data split was done at the very beginning of the analysis pipeline, prior to any processing step, or whether the data split was on the patient level, not the scan level.

Item19.reason: The study does not explicitly address the handling of confounding factors.

Item20.reason: The study reports AUC, accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity. ROC curves, calibration curves, and decision curve analysis are also presented.

Item21.reason: The study includes 95% confidence intervals (CI) for the AUC values.

Item22.reason: The study presents calibration curves for the models.

Item23.reason: The study does not perform uni-parametric evaluations to justify the need for a multi-parametric approach.

Item24.reason: The study compares the performance of the radiomics-based model with a clinical model based on LI-RADS features and other imaging features. It uses DeLong's test to compare the AUC values.

Item25.reason: The study does not compare the model with a simple baseline reference model.

Item26.reason: The model is tested on an internal validation cohort.

Item27.reason: The model is tested on an independent test cohort.

Item28.reason: The study does not mention any data availability.

Item29.reason: The study does not mention any code availability.

Item30.reason: The study does not mention any model availability.
